title: Daily Market Digest: Geopolitical Tensions & Weak Jobs Report Drive Market Lower
subtitle: Friday, March 7, 2026

items.headline: U.S. Stocks Tumble Amid Weak Jobs Data and Surging Oil Prices
items.summary: U.S. stock markets closed sharply lower on Friday, March 6, as an unexpected weakening in the labor market combined with a dramatic spike in oil prices to shake investor confidence. Geopolitical tensions in the Middle East intensified, leading to fears of renewed inflation and complicating the Federal Reserve's policy outlook. This marked the worst weekly performance for U.S. equities in months.

items.headline: Day One Biopharmaceuticals Surges on Strong Performance
items.summary: Day One Biopharmaceuticals ($DAWN) emerged as the biggest gainer, skyrocketing after reporting strong performance.

items.headline: Marvell Technology Posts Significant Gains
items.summary: Marvell Technology Inc. ($MRVL) saw a notable rise, following its record fiscal year 2026 revenue driven by AI demand, reported on March 5.

items.headline: Owlet Inc. Plunges, Leading Market Losers
items.summary: Owlet Inc. recorded the sharpest decline among major losers on Friday's session.

items.headline: NVIDIA Shares Decline
items.summary: NVIDIA ($NVDA) experienced a decline amidst broader market weakness.

items.headline: U.S. Job Market Under Strain as Employers Cut 92,000 Jobs in February
items.summary: American employers unexpectedly cut 92,000 jobs last month, contrary to economists' expectations of 60,000 new jobs. The unemployment rate increased to 4.4%, signaling a struggling labor market amid economic uncertainty.

items.headline: Oil Prices Surge Over 12% Amid Middle East Conflict
items.summary: Crude oil futures surged over 12% on March 6, with U.S. crude prices rising above $90 per barrel and Brent crude around $92 per barrel. The spike is driven by conflict-related disruption to shipping through the Strait of Hormuz, raising fears of renewed inflation.

items.headline: Federal Reserve Holds Rates Steady, Next Meeting in Mid-March
items.summary: The Federal Reserve maintained its benchmark interest rate at 3.5% to 3.75% at its January meeting. The next FOMC meeting is scheduled for March 17-18, where policymakers will assess fresh economic data, including inflation and employment trends, before making their next move.

items.headline: Consumer Discretionary Sector Lags Due to High Costs and Soft Demand
items.summary: The consumer discretionary sector is notably lagging, with a 5.3% decline in February. Companies in this sector are facing the steepest earnings slump since 2020, attributed to high operating costs and dampened consumer demand.

items.headline: Utilities and Energy Sectors Lead Gains in February
items.summary: In February, the Utilities sector advanced 10.2% and the Energy sector rose 9.4%, outperforming other sectors. This strength is partly attributed to rising electrical needs for data centers and the surge in oil prices.

items.headline: U.S. Treasury Considers Lifting More Russian Oil Sanctions Amid Price Surge
items.summary: U.S. Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent announced on Friday that the government is considering lifting sanctions on more Russian oil. This move comes as global oil prices surge, with measures aimed at bringing relief to the market during the ongoing conflict.
